searchForTile.name: Search for a Tile
searchForTile.description: Write a function that checks whether a specific letter tile is in the rack. Return true if found, false if not.

letterFoundAtStart.name: Letter at the start
letterFoundAtStart.description: The letter is the first tile in the rack.

letterFoundInMiddle.name: Letter in the middle
letterFoundInMiddle.description: The letter is in the middle of the rack.

letterFoundAtEnd.name: Letter at the end
letterFoundAtEnd.description: The letter is the last tile in the rack.

letterNotFound.name: Letter not found
letterNotFound.description: The letter isn't in the rack.

emptyRack.name: Empty rack
emptyRack.description: An empty rack has no tiles to find.

duplicateLetters.name: Rack with duplicates
duplicateLetters.description: The letter appears among duplicate tiles.

singleTileFound.name: Single tile found
singleTileFound.description: A rack with one tile that matches.

singleTileNotFound.name: Single tile not found
singleTileNotFound.description: A rack with one tile that doesn't match.

byHand.question: How would I do this by hand?
byHand.answer: You'd go through the haystack one element at a time, comparing each to the needle. As soon as you found a match you'd stop. If you got to the end without a match, the needle isn't there.

loopAndCompare.question: How do I loop and compare?
loopAndCompare.answer: A for-each loop gives you each element in turn. Inside, an if-statement compares the current element to the needle.

returnTrue.question: When should I return true?
returnTrue.answer: As soon as you find a match. There's no point checking the remaining elements.

returnFalse.question: When should I return false?
returnFalse.answer: ONLY after the loop has finished without finding a match. A common mistake is putting `return false` inside the loop. That gives up after the very first non-matching element, before you've checked the rest.
